title: LHC micro black holes: is the collider safe?
question: Could high-energy collisions at the LHC produce a microscopic black hole that endangers Earth?

sources.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group)
Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes
The Hierarchy Problem and New Dimensions at a Millimeter
Abraham-Solution to Schwarzschild Metric Implies That CERN Miniblack Holes Pose a Planetary Risk
Wagner & Sancho v. CERN et al. request to halt LHC startup over safety fears

passages.verbatimText: Nature has already conducted the equivalent of about a hundred thousand LHC experimental programmes on Earth — and the planet still exists.
There is no basis for any conceivable threat from the LHC.
Cosmic rays reach the Earth's atmosphere with energies far exceeding those of LHC collisions, and have done so throughout the history of the solar system.

The continued existence of white dwarfs and neutron stars, which would efficiently capture and be destroyed by any dangerous stable black holes, places strong empirical bounds excluding a risk to Earth.

Black holes produced by cosmic-ray collisions with a body at rest are highly relativistic and would traverse it, whereas black holes produced in symmetric LHC collisions could be slow enough to be gravitationally bound; we therefore consider capture by dense stellar remnants.
A black hole of mass M creates and emits particles as if it were a body with temperature proportional to its surface gravity, and thus slowly evaporates.
If there are large new spatial dimensions, the fundamental scale of gravity could be as low as the TeV scale, within reach of collider energies.
A modified solution to the Schwarzschild metric implies that microscopic black holes need not evaporate and could accrete matter, posing a planetary risk.

claims.statement: If large extra dimensions exist and the fundamental gravity scale is near a TeV, microscopic black holes could be produced at the LHC.
Producing black holes at the LHC requires speculative beyond-Standard-Model physics and is not predicted by established theory.
Any microscopic black hole would emit Hawking radiation and evaporate almost instantly.
Hawking radiation has never been directly observed experimentally.
caveats: A widely accepted theoretical prediction, but empirically unconfirmed at time of writing.

claims.statement: Cosmic rays strike Earth and other astronomical bodies at energies exceeding LHC collisions, and have for the lifetime of the solar system.
Cosmic-ray collisions have already performed the equivalent of many thousands of LHC programs on Earth, the Sun, and other bodies, which still exist.

structure.timeframe: over the age of the solar system

claims.statement: Cosmic-ray black holes would be produced at high velocity and escape a body, whereas LHC black holes could be produced nearly at rest and be gravitationally captured, so the cosmic-ray analogy is not automatically sufficient.
The survival of white dwarfs and neutron stars over gigayears bounds any producible stable black hole to harmless accretion rates, even accounting for capture of slow black holes.
The analysis covers both charged and neutral black holes, including the slowest-accreting neutral cases.
A modified Schwarzschild metric implies microscopic black holes could be stable and accrete dangerously.
caveats: Relies on a non-standard metric rejected by the mainstream physics community.

claims.statement: LHC collisions pose no credible danger of producing a black hole that could threaten Earth.

inferences.warrant: Objects that evaporate essentially instantly cannot accumulate or grow to a dangerous size.
defeaters: Hawking radiation does not exist or micro black holes are stable.

inferences.warrant: If the process were dangerous, higher-energy natural analogues over cosmological time would have already destroyed observed astronomical bodies; their survival bounds the risk.
defeaters: The at-rest capture disanalogy is not adequately covered by the stellar-remnant argument.

inferences.warrant: If any production requires speculative physics, the prior probability of producing a black hole at all is low.
If the natural analogue does not cover LHC-produced slow black holes, the empirical safety bound may not apply.
Dense stellar remnants capture even fast-moving black holes, so their survival addresses the at-rest concern.
A theoretical prediction never directly observed warrants reduced confidence in load-bearing use.
If micro black holes can be stable and accrete, LHC production could be dangerous.

challenges.rationale: Cosmic-ray black holes are relativistic and would traverse Earth, unlike potential at-rest LHC black holes, the natural analogue may not match the LHC case.
challenges.suggestedRemedy: Extend the argument to dense stellar remnants (white dwarfs, neutron stars) that capture even fast black holes.

challenges.rationale: Rests on a non-standard modification of the Schwarzschild metric that the physics community has rejected; not peer-reviewed.

target.id: direct laboratory observation of Hawking radiation

challenges.rationale: Line A leans on Hawking radiation, which was not directly observed at the time; the ledger should track whether the empirical line alone suffices.

correlationGroups.rationale: Both derive from the same Giddings–Mangano (2008) analysis; not independent evidence.

overlays.label: Mainstream physics consensus (LSAG)
overlays.description: The established view: safety is robustly supported by both the theoretical and empirical lines.
overlays.priorStance: LHC is safe with very high confidence.

overlays.label: Concerned skeptic
overlays.description: Distrusts the unobserved Hawking-radiation premise and worries the empirical bound may not cover at-rest capture.
overlays.priorStance: Residual doubt; wants the empirical line to stand without Hawking radiation.

assessments.rationale: A real subtlety, but fully addressed by the remnant analysis.
Both charged and neutral cases are covered.
Never directly observed; unwilling to make it load-bearing.
Powerful if the capture analysis for slow black holes is complete.
The at-rest vs relativistic disanalogy is the crux.
Unsure the slow-neutral capture analysis is complete.

sourceDocument.title: LHC micro black holes: is the collider safe? (assembled source passages)
sourceDocument.text:
LHC micro black holes: is the collider safe?

Assembled from the source passages this case was built on. Each section is verbatim text drawn from the cited source; ordering follows the sources listed in the case.

## Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions (LHC Safety Assessment Group)
J. Ellis, G. Giudice, M. Mangano, I. Tkachev, U. Wiedemann; 2008-09-05

Nature has already conducted the equivalent of about a hundred thousand LHC experimental programmes on Earth — and the planet still exists. (§Cosmic rays)

There is no basis for any conceivable threat from the LHC. (§Conclusions)

Cosmic rays reach the Earth's atmosphere with energies far exceeding those of LHC collisions, and have done so throughout the history of the solar system. (§Cosmic rays)

## Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes
S. Giddings, M. Mangano; 2008-06-20

The continued existence of white dwarfs and neutron stars, which would efficiently capture and be destroyed by any dangerous stable black holes, places strong empirical bounds excluding a risk to Earth.

Black holes produced by cosmic-ray collisions with a body at rest are highly relativistic and would traverse it, whereas black holes produced in symmetric LHC collisions could be slow enough to be gravitationally bound; we therefore consider capture by dense stellar remnants.

## Particle creation by black holes
S. W. Hawking; 1975-08-01

A black hole of mass M creates and emits particles as if it were a body with temperature proportional to its surface gravity, and thus slowly evaporates.

## The Hierarchy Problem and New Dimensions at a Millimeter
N. Arkani-Hamed, S. Dimopoulos, G. Dvali; 1998-03-11

If there are large new spatial dimensions, the fundamental scale of gravity could be as low as the TeV scale, within reach of collider energies.

## Abraham-Solution to Schwarzschild Metric Implies That CERN Miniblack Holes Pose a Planetary Risk
O. Rössler; 2008-01-01

A modified solution to the Schwarzschild metric implies that microscopic black holes need not evaporate and could accrete matter, posing a planetary risk. (§Abstract)
